claim: Cross-App Access (XAA) using the Identity Assertion Authorization Grant (ID Jag) flow eliminates repeated OAuth consent screens by using a single SSO login across all MCP servers.
actionable: Configure your MCP client (Cursor, Claude Code) to support SSO + ID Jag token exchange; ensure your identity provider (Okta, Entra) supports XAA-compatible connections.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: XAA requires trust between three systems: MCP client, MCP server, and Identity Provider; the IDP acts as a trust broker to issue short-lived access tokens without human intervention.
actionable: When implementing XAA for your MCP servers, ensure all three parties (client, server, IDP) are registered in your identity provider and have explicit trust relationships configured.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: MCP servers using XAA should issue short-lived access tokens (5 minutes) that expire immediately if the user's SSO session is revoked, improving security posture over long-lived API keys.
actionable: Configure your MCP server to issue 5-minute access tokens via XAA; require ID Jag re-exchange for renewal, ensuring instant access revocation when SSO is disabled.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: Okta currently supports XAA for OIDC-based connections; Microsoft Entra does not yet support XAA, but RFC 9728 standardizes XAA across all OIDC-compliant identity providers.
actionable: If using Okta, enable XAA connections immediately; if using Entra, track RFC 9728 adoption and plan migration once support is announced.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: IT teams cannot currently audit or control which MCP servers users connect to because MCP uses OAuth in isolation; XAA enables centralized policy control via the identity provider.
actionable: Implement XAA to give IT teams visibility: set up managed connections in your IDP to specify which MCP clients can request access to which servers.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: MCP servers implementing XAA need to support JWT bearer token validation by checking the token signature against the identity provider's public key.
actionable: Add JWT validation to your MCP server: fetch the IDP's public key, verify the JWT signature, and validate the 'audience' claim matches your server's identifier.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: WorkOS provides authentication services for Cursor and Anthropic's SSO implementations, making them XAA-compatible today without additional configuration.
actionable: If building MCP clients, consider using WorkOS for SSO + XAA support; Cursor and Claude Code already have this integration available.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS

claim: XAA solves authentication but not authorization; scope-based permissions still require additional configuration at the identity provider or MCP server level.
actionable: When designing XAA for MCP, plan scope-based authorization separately; XAA handles 'is this user authenticated' but not 'what can this user do in this service'.
source_title: One Login to Rule Them All: Cross-App Access for MCP — Garrett Galow, WorkOS
